explanation: Daniel 6:3 records that 'Daniel became distinguished above all the other high officials and satraps, because an excellent spirit was in him, and the king planned to set him over the whole kingdom.' This excellent spirit refers to Daniel's exceptional wisdom, integrity, and God-given ability that set him apart from his peers administratively, not just religiously.

subject: CRS
text: Daniel could not be harmed by the lions because

options.A: an angel shut the mouths of the lions
options.C: a pillar of cloud separated Daniel from the lions

explanation: Daniel 6:22 records Daniel's own testimony to the king: 'My God sent his angel and shut the lions' mouths, and they have not harmed me, because I was found blameless before him.' God supernaturally protected Daniel through angelic intervention, not through natural means. This miracle confirmed Daniel's innocence and led Darius to issue a decree honoring Daniel's God.

subject: CRS
text: The Israelites did evil in the eyes of the LORD in the days of the Judges by
explanation: Judges 2:11 states, 'And the people of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the LORD and served the Baals.' The cyclical pattern in Judges consistently identifies Baal worship as the primary evil Israel committed. The Baals were Canaanite fertility gods, and Israel's adoption of this worship was the central covenant violation that triggered God's judgment and foreign oppression.

subject: CRS
text: Selling the needy for a pair of shoes in Amos means

options.B: equating human beings with material things

explanation: Amos 2:6 declares that Israel 'sell the righteous for silver, and the needy for a pair of sandals.' This prophetic indictment refers to the practice of selling poor debtors into slavery for trivial amounts — even the price of a pair of sandals. The underlying meaning is the systematic oppression of the poor by the powerful wealthy class, which violated God's covenant requirements for justice.

subject: CRS
text: 'Thus says the LORD: For three transgressions of Israel and for four, I will not revoke the punishment...' In the latter part of this prophecy, Amos condemned Israel for

options.A: not remembering the covenant of brotherhood
options.D: rejecting the laws and statutes of the LORD

explanation: Amos 2:6-8 details Israel's specific sins: selling the righteous for silver, oppressing the poor, sexual immorality at altars, and drinking wine bought with unjust fines. These charges collectively represent a complete neglect of social justice — Israel had abandoned every obligation to protect the vulnerable in society. Amos's oracle against Israel is the climax of his series of national indictments.

subject: CRS
text: The central theme of Isaiah's teachings is that

options.D: the vilest sinner will not be redeemed

explanation: Isaiah's overarching message combines judgment with hope and restoration. Throughout the book, Isaiah proclaims that despite Israel's deep sin (Isaiah 1:18 — 'though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow'), God offers redemption. The servant songs and promises of restoration in Isaiah 40-66 confirm that no sinner is beyond God's redemptive reach, making C the central theme.

subject: CRS
text: 'Whom shall I send, and who will go for us? Then I said, Here am I! Send me.' Why did Isaiah offer himself to be God's messenger?

options.A: Because the king had died and he needed another job
options.B: Having been cleansed of his sins, he saw the need to warn others
options.C: Because he had seen the beauty of heaven and wanted to work for God

explanation: In Isaiah 6, the prophet's call narrative shows that Isaiah was first overwhelmed by his own sinfulness before the holy God, crying 'Woe is me! For I am lost; for I am a man of unclean lips.' After a seraph touched his lips with a burning coal and declared his guilt taken away (Isaiah 6:6-7), Isaiah was then moved to volunteer when he heard God's call. His willingness arose from having received cleansing and feeling compelled to warn others.

subject: CRS
text: Who, according to Ezekiel, did the exiles blame for their plight?
explanation: Ezekiel 18:2 quotes the proverb the exiles were using: 'The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge.' The exiles blamed their fathers' generation for the sins that led to the Babylonian exile, claiming they were suffering for wrongs they did not personally commit. Ezekiel's message was to refute this and establish individual moral accountability.

subject: CRS
text: God told Jeremiah that He would give Jerusalem into the hands of the Chaldeans because the Israelites

options.C: did not prepare adequately for war

explanation: In Jeremiah 32:28-32, God declared that He would give Jerusalem into the hands of the Chaldeans because 'the people of Israel and the people of Judah have done nothing but evil in my sight from their youth. The people of Israel have done nothing but provoke me to anger.' The fall of Jerusalem was God's judgment in response to persistent covenant violation and idolatry.

subject: CRS
text: The expression 'dry bones' in Ezekiel refers to
explanation: Ezekiel 37:11 provides the explicit divine interpretation: 'Then he said to me, Son of man, these bones are the whole house of Israel. Behold, they say, Our bones are dried up, and our hope is lost; we are indeed cut off.' The vision of the valley of dry bones in Ezekiel 37 is an allegory of national restoration — God would revive the exiled and despairing nation of Israel.

subject: CRS
text: According to Paul, the law and the prophets bear witness to the
explanation: Romans 3:21 states, 'But now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law, although the Law and the Prophets bear witness to it.' Paul argues that the entire Old Testament — Law and Prophets — pointed to and testified about God's righteousness, which is now fully revealed through faith in Jesus Christ apart from works of the law.

subject: CRS
text: According to Paul in his Letter to the Galatians, one is justified by
explanation: Galatians 2:16 is explicit: 'yet we know that a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ, so we also have believed in Christ Jesus, in order to be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law.' Paul emphasizes this truth three times in the same verse, making faith in Jesus Christ the sole basis for justification before God.

subject: CRS
text: The function of the law to believers before the coming of Christ was that of a
explanation: Galatians 3:24 states, 'So then, the law was our guardian (custodian/schoolmaster) until Christ came, in order that we might be justified by faith.' The Greek word 'paidagogos' refers to a slave who accompanied children to school and maintained discipline — a temporary guardian role. Paul uses this image to show the law's preparatory and provisional function before Christ fulfilled it.

subject: CRS
text: Justification by grace shows that work is
explanation: Romans 11:6 states, 'But if it is by grace, it is no longer on the basis of works; otherwise grace would no longer be grace.' Paul's doctrine of justification by grace through faith (Ephesians 2:8-9) establishes that salvation is entirely God's gift, not earned through human effort. If works were required, grace would be negated as the operative principle of salvation.

subject: CRS
text: All Christians who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized
explanation: Romans 6:3 asks, 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?' Paul teaches that Christian baptism is a participation in Christ's death and resurrection. Just as Christ died and rose, the baptized believer dies to sin and rises to new life, making baptism a declaration of union with Christ's redemptive work.

subject: CRS
text: In what way did God condemn sin in the flesh?

options.A: He allowed Jesus to be crucified
options.B: Repentance was made possible for the forgiveness of sin
options.C: Resurrection of the flesh rendered sin ineffective
options.D: He sent His own Son in the likeness of man

explanation: Romans 8:3 states, 'For God has done what the law, weakened by the flesh, could not do. By sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh and for sin, he condemned sin in the flesh.' God's method of condemning sin was the Incarnation — sending His Son in human form to deal with sin from within humanity itself, accomplishing what external law could not achieve.

subject: CRS
text: 'But you are not in darkness brethren, for that day to surprise you like a thief.' That 'day' refers to the
explanation: 1 Thessalonians 5:2-4 uses 'that day' in the context of the 'day of the Lord' coming like a thief in the night — a reference to the Second Coming of Christ. Paul contrasts those in spiritual darkness who will be surprised with believers who, as 'children of light,' are watchful and prepared for the Lord's return. The coming of the LORD (Second Coming/Parousia) is the clear referent.

subject: CRS
text: The main aim of the teaching of Christ's humility in Paul's Letter to the Philippians is that every Christian should

options.A: regard himself as equal to Jesus because He came in human form
options.B: confess that Jesus Christ is LORD to the glory of God the Father
options.C: work out his own salvation with fear and trembling
options.D: look into the interest of others and do nothing from selfishness

explanation: Philippians 2:3-4 introduces the Christ hymn with this practical exhortation: 'Do nothing from selfish ambition or conceit, but in humility count others more significant than yourselves. Let each of you look not only to his own interests, but also to the interests of others.' The Christ hymn (vv. 5-11) is given as the supreme example of this selfless, other-focused humility that Paul urges believers to imitate.

subject: CRS
text: According to Paul's Letter to the Philippians, God exalted Jesus and gave Him a name which is above all names because He

options.B: was humble and obedient unto death
options.D: was the greatest man ever born

explanation: Philippians 2:8-9 records the pattern of humiliation and exaltation: 'And being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' The exaltation is explicitly stated as the consequence of Christ's radical humility and obedience, even to the cross.

subject: CRS
text: In the Letter to the Galatians, Christians are encouraged to bear one another's burdens in order to
explanation: Galatians 6:2 states, 'Bear one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ.' Paul identifies mutual burden-bearing as the means of fulfilling Christ's law — which he elsewhere summarizes as the law of love (Galatians 5:14). This communal responsibility embodies the selfless love that Christ exemplified and commanded, replacing the external Mosaic law with an inward principle of love-driven service.

subject: CRS
text: 'Be angry but do not sin' means that anger

options.B: it is not a sin at all
options.D: could be an occasion for sin

explanation: Ephesians 4:26 quotes Psalm 4:4: 'Be angry and do not sin; do not let the sun go down on your anger, and give no opportunity to the devil' (v.27). The text acknowledges that anger itself may not always be sinful (even Jesus expressed righteous anger), but it warns that anger unchecked creates opportunity for sin. The practical instruction — resolve anger before sunset — shows anger can become a dangerous occasion for sinful behavior.

subject: CRS
text: There are varieties of gifts and so different people have different gifts. Who gives all these gifts?
explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:11 states, 'All these are empowered by one and the same Spirit, who apportions to each one individually as he wills.' Paul's entire argument in 1 Corinthians 12 is that despite the diversity of spiritual gifts (charismata), they all have a single source: the Holy Spirit. The Spirit sovereignly distributes gifts according to His own will for the benefit of the whole body.

subject: CRS
text: The overriding principle in the use of spiritual gifts is
explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:40 concludes Paul's extended discussion on spiritual gifts, especially tongues and prophecy, with the rule: 'But all things should be done decently and in order.' After addressing abuse of tongues and the need for intelligibility and edification (chapters 12-14), Paul's summary principle for governing all spiritual gifts in corporate worship is orderliness and propriety.

subject: CRS
text: For the administration of the churches, Paul directed that Christians should

options.A: surrender all they have to the church

explanation: In 1 Corinthians 16:1-2, Paul directed: 'Now concerning the collection for the saints: as I directed the churches of Galatia, so you also are to do. On the first day of every week, each of you is to put something aside and store it up.' This instruction established a pattern of regular, systematic, weekly giving for the support of the church's ministry and relief of needy believers.

subject: CRS
text: Contribution of relief materials for the needy in the church according to Paul, must be
explanation: 2 Corinthians 9:7 states, 'Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.' Paul emphasizes that Christian giving must be voluntary and joyful, not coerced or grudging. The cheerful giver reflects God's own generous and willing character, and giving done in this spirit is what God accepts and blesses.

subject: CRS
text: Paul teaches that we should obey governing authorities in order to avoid

options.D: God's wrath and a bad conscience

explanation: Romans 13:5 gives the dual motivation for obeying authorities: 'Therefore one must be in subjection, not only to avoid God's wrath but also for the sake of conscience.' Paul presents civil obedience not merely as prudent self-preservation but as a matter of theological necessity — resisting God-ordained authority invites divine wrath — and personal integrity, since a clear conscience is essential to Christian character.

subject: CRS
text: When Paul says 'if anyone will not work, let him not eat,' he is saying that

options.B: the lazy person must eat only a little
options.C: everyone should toil and labour with his hands
options.D: every person should seek lucrative job

explanation: 2 Thessalonians 3:10-12 addresses idle members of the Thessalonian church who had stopped working, apparently expecting the imminent Second Coming. Paul's instruction — 'if anyone is not willing to work, let him not eat' — is a call for everyone to engage in productive labour. The point is not about the involuntarily unemployed but about the idle who refuse to work, establishing the principle that every able person should toil with their hands.

subject: CRS
text: Paul worked harder than any of the apostles because

options.A: he had the strength to do so
options.B: of the grace of God that was with him
options.C: Jesus personally revealed Himself to him
options.D: he was formerly a persecutor of the church

explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:10 records Paul's testimony: 'But by the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace toward me was not in vain. On the contrary, I worked harder than any of them, though it was not I, but the grace of God that is with me.' Paul explicitly attributes his extraordinary apostolic labor not to personal ability but to God's empowering grace working through him.

subject: CRS
text: Christian wives are to be subject to their husbands

options.A: since their husbands take care of them
options.B: because it is fitting in the LORD
options.C: because it is the natural thing to do
options.D: since women are not expected to be heard

explanation: Colossians 3:18 instructs: 'Wives, submit to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord.' The ground for this submission is theological — it is the appropriate Christian response 'in the Lord,' meaning within the framework of Christian discipleship and covenant life. This is not based on social convention or economic dependence but on what aligns with life under Christ's lordship.

subject: CRS
text: On being revealed at the coming of Christ, the lawless will be slain by the
explanation: 2 Thessalonians 2:8 declares, 'And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will kill with the breath of his mouth and bring to nothing by the appearance of his coming.' Paul describes the destruction of the man of lawlessness (antichrist) by the effortless power of Christ's word at His Second Coming — a breath suffices to annihilate the greatest enemy, showing Christ's absolute supremacy.

subject: CRS
text: An important feature of the second coming of Jesus as taught by Paul is the

options.B: establishment of a world government under Christ

explanation: 1 Corinthians 15 is Paul's extended treatment of the resurrection, and 1 Thessalonians 4:16 describes the Second Coming: 'For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first.' The resurrection of the dead is the central and defining feature of Christ's return in Pauline eschatology.

subject: CRS
text: 'What you sow does not come to life unless it dies?' This statement refers to the

options.B: good works which are expected of the Christian
options.D: reward of the works of Christians

explanation: 1 Corinthians 15:36-37 uses the agricultural analogy of a seed to explain bodily resurrection: 'You foolish person! What you sow does not come to life unless it dies. And what you sow is not the body that is to be, but a bare kernel.' Paul employs this illustration to answer the question of how the dead are raised and in what kind of body they come — the seed must die before it can produce new life, analogous to the resurrection body.

subject: CRS
text: Paul's experience as recorded in his second Letter to the Corinthians, teaches that the attitude of Christians to suffering should be that of
explanation: In 2 Corinthians 12:7-10, Paul describes his 'thorn in the flesh' — a persistent suffering he prayed three times to have removed. God's response was, 'My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.' Paul's conclusion was to boast in his weaknesses so that Christ's power might rest on him. This models perseverance through suffering, finding strength in God's grace rather than seeking escape.

subject: CRS
text: In the Letter to the Hebrews, despite the victory through faith in God of the men of old, they did not receive what was promised. What was this promise?
explanation: Hebrews 11:39-40 concludes the faith chapter: 'And all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised, since God had provided something better for us, that apart from us they should not be made perfect.' The 'something better' provided for believers in the New Covenant era is Christ Himself — His atoning work, priesthood, and covenant — which the Old Testament saints anticipated but did not live to receive.

subject: CRS
text: Abraham's faith and works were better illustrated when he

options.A: took Lot with him to Canaan
options.B: offered his son, Isaac, on the altar

explanation: James 2:21-22 uses Abraham's offering of Isaac as the supreme example of faith working together with works: 'Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up his son Isaac on the altar? You see that faith was active along with his works, and faith was completed by his works.' Hebrews 11:17-19 also highlights this event as the pinnacle of Abraham's faith, showing that genuine faith is demonstrated by costly obedient action.

subject: CRS
text: 'Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for... For by it the men of old received divine approval.' Who are the men of old mentioned as examples of faith in the Letter to the Hebrews?
explanation: Hebrews 11 — the 'faith hall of fame' — explicitly names Abel (v.4), Abraham (v.8, 17), and Rahab (v.31) as prime examples of faith. Abel is listed first as the earliest exemplar, Abraham as the forefather of faith, and Rahab the harlot as a surprising inclusion showing faith crosses all boundaries. The other options contain figures not featured as faith examples in this chapter.

subject: CRS
text: According to James, effective prayer is that which is offered
explanation: James 5:15-16 states, 'And the prayer of faith will save the one who is sick... The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working.' James identifies faith as the operative element that makes prayer effective. The example of Elijah (v.17-18) illustrates that fervent, faith-filled prayer produces results — he prayed earnestly and rain was withheld and then restored, not because of emotional intensity or vocal volume but because of faith.

subject: CRS
text: According to Peter, Christians living among non-Christians should
explanation: 1 Peter 2:12 instructs, 'Keep your conduct among the Gentiles honorable, so that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may see your good deeds and glorify God on the day of visitation.' Peter's strategy for Christian witness in a hostile society is maintaining exemplary moral conduct. Good behavior serves both as a refutation of slander and as a testimony that can lead unbelievers to glorify God.

subject: CRS
text: Peter enjoins Christians to be good citizens by

options.A: using their freedom to fight for their right

explanation: 1 Peter 2:13-14 commands, 'Be subject for the Lord's sake to every human institution, whether it be to the emperor as supreme, or to governors as sent by him to punish those who do evil and to praise those who do good.' Peter grounds civic obedience in theological motivation ('for the Lord's sake') and views proper submission to civil authority as an expression of Christian good citizenship and witness in the world.

subject: CRS
text: 'Let us make man in our image...' In the statement above, 'man' refers to
explanation: Genesis 1:27 clarifies the meaning: 'So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.' The Hebrew word 'adam' in the creation account is used generically for humanity — both male and female together bear the image of God. The text explicitly states that both genders were created in God's image, making 'male and female' the correct answer.

subject: CRS
text: In extolling the sovereignty of God, Amos contrasted Him with the Nile, which he described as

options.C: the source of the waters of the sea

explanation: Amos 8:8 and 9:5 describe the Nile as rising and sinking (or rising like the Nile and subsiding again) to portray God's power over nature. Amos uses this imagery to contrast the unstable, cyclical nature of the Nile with the sovereign, unchanging power of God. The rising and sinking of the Nile was well known to ancient peoples as a dramatic natural phenomenon.

subject: CRS
text: By keeping God's covenant, the people of Israel would become

options.B: a kingdom of priests to God
options.D: a nation where God would manifest Himself

explanation: Exodus 19:5-6 records God's promise that if Israel would obey His voice and keep His covenant, they would be His treasured possession and become 'a kingdom of priests and a holy nation.' This priestly role meant Israel was to serve as mediators between God and the rest of the world. The covenant relationship came with both privilege and responsibility.

subject: CRS
text: According to Jeremiah, the vital element in the new covenant God would make with the Jews was that

options.A: He would bless all nations through the Jews
options.B: Man would be unable to keep God's law
options.C: salvation would come through a virgin's seed
options.D: the individual would be accountable before God

explanation: Jeremiah 31:29-34 describes the new covenant in which God would write His law on the hearts of individuals. This covenant moves away from collective responsibility (fathers eating sour grapes and children's teeth being set on edge) to individual accountability before God. Each person would know God directly, not through inherited tradition alone.

subject: CRS
text: Joseph saw his rise to leadership position in Egypt in relation to his brothers as

options.A: a proof of his superiority over them
options.B: an opportunity to avenge their evil deeds
options.C: a time to test their honesty
options.D: a means of preserving their lives

explanation: Genesis 45:7 records Joseph telling his brothers that God sent him ahead of them to preserve for them a remnant on earth and to save their lives. Joseph interpreted his suffering and elevation not as personal triumph or revenge but as God's providential plan to preserve the family during the famine. This reflects his mature, faith-based perspective on his trials.

subject: CRS
text: "If you will go with me, I will go; but if you will not go with me, I will not go." In the statement above, "you" refers to
explanation: Judges 4:8 records Barak saying to Deborah the prophetess that he would only go to fight Sisera if she accompanied him. Barak's condition demonstrates both his dependence on Deborah's prophetic guidance and his reluctance to face the battle alone. Deborah agreed but warned that the glory of victory would go to a woman because of his hesitation.

subject: CRS
text: The name Ichabod means
explanation: 1 Samuel 4:21 records that Eli's daughter-in-law named her newborn son Ichabod, saying 'The glory has departed from Israel,' because the ark of God had been captured by the Philistines. The name Ichabod literally means 'no glory' or 'the glory has departed.' This marked a tragic turning point in Israel's spiritual history.

subject: CRS
text: How did David perceive that his child from Bathsheba was dead?

options.A: Bathsheba lamented the loss of her child
options.B: He saw his servants whispering together
options.C: He received a message to that effect from Joab
options.D: God revealed it to him through Nathan

explanation: 2 Samuel 12:19 records that David noticed his servants were whispering among themselves and perceived from this that the child was dead. He then asked directly and they confirmed it. David's astute observation of his servants' behaviour showed his perceptiveness; he had been praying and fasting for the child's recovery and was alert to any sign of change.

subject: CRS
text: "As one falls before the wicked you have fallen." This is a part of David's Lamentation over
explanation: 2 Samuel 3:33-34 records David's lament over Abner, who had been treacherously killed by Joab. David lamented that Abner did not die as a criminal deserves to die but fell as one falls before the wicked. This lamentation was David's public declaration that he was innocent of Abner's blood and that Joab acted without his knowledge or consent.

subject: CRS
text: The mother of the living child asked Solomon to give the child to the other woman because

options.A: killing the child would serve no useful purpose
options.B: her heart yearned for the child
options.C: she was confident that the king would ensure justice
options.D: she believed the child would recognize her at maturity

explanation: 1 Kings 3:26 records that when Solomon ordered the living child to be divided, the real mother's heart yearned for her son and she pleaded that the child be given to the other woman rather than killed. This maternal compassion and self-sacrifice revealed her as the true mother. Solomon's wisdom in discerning the true mother became legendary throughout Israel.

subject: CRS
text: Hosea's matrimonial experience symbolizes God's intention to

options.A: reform Israel and make her greater

explanation: Hosea's marriage to the unfaithful Gomer mirrors God's relationship with unfaithful Israel. Just as Hosea took back his wayward wife, God intended to woo Israel back and redeem her from her spiritual harlotry. The prophet's personal experience became a living parable of God's redemptive love and His intention to restore the broken covenant relationship with Israel.

subject: CRS
text: The harlotry of Gomer is compared to Israel's
explanation: In the Book of Hosea, Gomer's physical unfaithfulness to Hosea is used as a direct allegory for Israel's spiritual unfaithfulness to God. Israel had abandoned the Lord to worship the Baals and other foreign gods, breaking the covenant relationship just as Gomer broke her marriage vows. This powerful metaphor emphasised that idolatry is spiritual adultery.

subject: CRS
text: "Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory." This was said when

options.D: David brought the ark to Jerusalem

explanation: Isaiah 6:1-3 records that Isaiah received his vision in the year that King Uzziah died, during which the seraphim sang 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts.' The death of the earthly king provided the backdrop for Isaiah's vision of the heavenly King enthroned in glory. This triumphal hymn of the angels emphasised God's transcendent holiness and sovereignty over all creation.

subject: CRS
text: "Hear the word of the LORD, you rulers of Sodom! Give ear to the teaching of our God, you people of Gomorrah!" The nation referred to as Sodom and Gomorrah in the statement above is
explanation: Isaiah 1:10 addresses the rulers and people of Judah (Jerusalem) as 'rulers of Sodom' and 'people of Gomorrah.' Isaiah ministered primarily to Judah and Jerusalem, using the infamous names of Sodom and Gomorrah to shock his audience into recognising their moral and spiritual degradation. The comparison to these destroyed cities was a severe indictment of Judah's wickedness.

subject: CRS
text: Jeremiah was not anxious to accept God's call to become a prophet because

options.B: he knew that people would not listen to him
options.C: he needed to know God more
options.D: he was too young and inexperienced

explanation: Jeremiah 1:6 records Jeremiah's response to God's call: 'Ah, Lord God! Behold, I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth.' Jeremiah's reluctance stemmed from his sense of personal inadequacy due to his youth and inexperience. God reassured him that He would put His words in Jeremiah's mouth and that He had set him apart before he was even born.

subject: CRS
text: The devil's principal purpose for tempting Jesus Christ was to

options.A: show that nobody is above temptation
options.B: demonstrate that he controls the world
options.C: lure Jesus Christ from his mission
options.D: prove that after all, Jesus is only human

explanation: The devil's primary aim in the three temptations was to divert Jesus from His God-given mission of redemption through sacrifice. Each temptation offered an alternative path — provision without dependence on God, power without the cross, and kingship through compromise. Satan sought to lure Jesus away from the path of suffering servanthood that the Father had appointed for Him.

subject: CRS
text: "Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests; but the son of man has nowhere to lay his head." The statement above implies that

options.B: the son of man needs no house

explanation: Jesus made this statement in response to someone who declared he would follow Jesus wherever He went. By pointing out that He had nowhere to lay His head, Jesus was warning that following Him requires sacrifice and total commitment, not comfort and security. True discipleship demands a willingness to give up worldly comforts, which is why discipleship costs much.

subject: CRS
text: According to Luke, following Jesus involves
explanation: Luke 9:23 records Jesus saying that if anyone would come after Him, he must deny himself, take up his cross daily, and follow Him. Self-denial — the renunciation of one's own will and desires in favour of God's will — is the central requirement Jesus places on discipleship in Luke's Gospel. This radical commitment distinguishes genuine disciples from mere admirers.

subject: CRS
text: In the healing ministry of Jesus Christ, which of the following played a very vital role?
explanation: Throughout the healing narratives in the Gospels, faith is consistently highlighted as the vital element. Jesus frequently said 'your faith has made you well' or 'according to your faith be it done to you.' In His hometown of Nazareth, He could do few miracles because of the people's unbelief (Mark 6:5-6). Faith was the channel through which healing power flowed from Jesus to the individual.

subject: CRS
text: Jesus healed the only son of the widow of Nain because

options.B: the widow demonstrated faith in Him
options.D: he wanted to make people believe in Him

explanation: Luke 7:13 explicitly states that 'when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her and said to her, Do not weep.' The widow made no request and did not demonstrate faith; Jesus acted purely out of His spontaneous compassion for her grief. This miracle uniquely shows Jesus taking the initiative in response to human sorrow, without any prompting or request from the recipient.

subject: CRS
text: The healing of the paralytic brought by his four friends demonstrates

options.A: the power of Jesus to heal all types of diseases
options.B: that the friends had faith in the healing power of Jesus
options.C: the compassion of Jesus on the sick man
options.D: that Jesus has power to forgive sin

explanation: Mark 2:10 records Jesus saying 'But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins...' before commanding the paralytic to rise and walk. The healing was performed specifically to demonstrate Jesus' divine authority to forgive sins, which the scribes considered blasphemy. The miracle thus validated His claim to a power that belongs to God alone.

subject: CRS
text: According to Mark and Luke, the response of blind Bartimaeus to healing was to

options.B: run to show himself to the priests
options.D: speak to the Jews about Jesus

explanation: Mark 10:52 records that immediately after receiving his sight, Bartimaeus followed Jesus on the road. This immediate response of discipleship stands in contrast to some other healed individuals who went their own way. Bartimaeus demonstrated that genuine faith leads not only to receiving God's blessing but also to committed following of Jesus as Lord.

subject: CRS
text: The parable which teaches about God's judgment is that of the
explanation: Matthew 13:24-30 and 36-43 presents the parable of the wheat and weeds (tares) which specifically teaches about God's final judgment. Jesus explains that at the end of the age, the Son of Man will send His angels to separate the righteous from the wicked, with the wicked thrown into the fiery furnace. No other parable in this list so explicitly addresses divine eschatological judgment.

subject: CRS
text: The parable of the good Samaritan implies that

options.B: Priests and Levites hardly show love
options.C: neighbourliness is expressed in love and concern
options.D: a good neighbour must show love to his enemies

explanation: The parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) redefines neighbourliness — it is not determined by ethnicity or religious status but by practical love and concern in action. The Samaritan showed himself a true neighbour through his compassionate response to the injured man's need. Jesus' closing command to 'go and do likewise' emphasises that love and concern for others in need is the essence of being a neighbour.

subject: CRS
text: Luke ends the sermon on the plain with an analogy of a building to emphasize the importance of

options.D: preaching and abiding by the word

explanation: Luke 6:47-49 records Jesus concluding the sermon by contrasting the person who hears His words and does them (like a house built on a rock) with the person who hears but does not do them (like a house without foundation). The analogy specifically stresses hearing combined with obeying — not merely intellectual knowledge or preaching, but practical obedience to what one has heard.

subject: CRS
text: Jesus implies in His teaching on adultery, prayer, alms and fasting that these are more of matters of
explanation: In Matthew 5-6, Jesus consistently redirects attention from outward religious performance to inward motivation of the heart. Whether discussing lust (adultery in the heart), prayer (not for public show), almsgiving (done in secret), or fasting (without public display), Jesus teaches that God sees and judges the heart's intentions. True righteousness is a matter of the inner life, not external compliance.

subject: CRS
text: Jesus' injunction not to lay up treasure on earth for ourselves implies that the Christian should
explanation: Jesus' teaching in Matthew 6:19-20 to lay up treasures in heaven rather than on earth calls Christians to invest in eternal values through good works, generosity and righteousness. Paul echoes this in 1 Timothy 6:17-19, instructing the rich to be rich in good deeds and to be generous, thereby storing up treasure as a good foundation for the coming age. The emphasis is on heavenly investment through righteous living.

subject: CRS
text: The seventy disciples who returned from their missionary work were told by Jesus to rejoice because

options.A: they were well received by people
options.B: the spirits were subject to them
options.C: their names were written in heaven

explanation: Luke 10:20 records Jesus telling the returning seventy not to rejoice that spirits were subject to them, but to rejoice that their names were written in heaven. Jesus redirected their excitement from spiritual power and ministry success to the greater and more foundational reality of their personal salvation and standing before God. Eternal life registered in God's book is a greater cause for joy than any miracle.

subject: CRS
text: According to Matthew, when Jesus was transfigured

options.A: his face shone like the sun
options.B: his body became as white as light
options.C: he could only communicate with heaven

explanation: Matthew 17:2 specifically records that during the transfiguration, 'his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light.' While Mark and Luke emphasise the brightness of His garments, Matthew's distinctive detail is that Jesus' face shone like the sun. This radiance revealed the divine glory that Jesus possessed and anticipates the glory He will have at His second coming.

subject: CRS
text: According to Matthew, Jesus entered Jerusalem riding
explanation: Matthew 21:7 records that the disciples brought both an ass (donkey) and a colt, and placed their cloaks on them for Jesus to ride. Matthew's account uniquely mentions two animals, quoting Zechariah 9:9 which speaks of a donkey and a colt. The disciples set garments on both animals, and Jesus rode into Jerusalem fulfilling this prophecy of the humble king.

subject: CRS
text: The prayer of Jesus before his arrest showed that

options.B: his disciples had not understood him thoroughly
options.C: he was not prepared to die on the cross
options.D: he wanted his disciples to be prayerful

explanation: Mark 14:33-35 records that in Gethsemane, Jesus began to be greatly distressed and troubled, and He said to His disciples that His soul was 'very sorrowful, even to death.' This prayer of anguish — 'Abba, Father, all things are possible for you. Remove this cup from me' — reveals Jesus' genuine human emotion of sorrow and grief as He faced the cross. Despite this, He submitted to the Father's will.

subject: CRS
text: "Are you the king of the Jews?" Jesus' reply to the question above by Pilate was

options.B: "My kingship is not of this world"
options.C: "Who has made you a prince and a judge over us?"
options.D: "Why put me to the test, you hypocrites?"

explanation: Matthew 27:11 records that when Pilate asked Jesus, 'Are you the King of the Jews?' Jesus replied, 'You have said so.' This non-committal yet affirmative response neither denied His kingship nor framed it in a way that would confirm the political charge against Him. In John's Gospel, Jesus elaborates that His kingdom is not of this world, but Matthew simply records the brief affirmative reply.

subject: CRS
text: According to Matthew, when the women went to see the sepulchre, they

options.A: found that the disciples had been there
options.B: saw an angel sitting upon the stone
options.D: found the guards rolling back the stone

explanation: Matthew 28:2-3 records that an angel of the Lord descended and rolled back the stone from the tomb and sat upon it; his appearance was like lightning and his clothing white as snow. When the women arrived, they encountered this angel sitting on the rolled-away stone. Matthew's account uniquely describes the angel sitting on the stone outside the tomb, distinct from other Gospel accounts.

subject: CRS
text: Jesus used the illustration of the vine to teach that a Christian that abides in Him will surely
explanation: John 15:5 records Jesus saying, 'I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.' The vine illustration teaches that fruitfulness in the Christian life is entirely dependent on maintaining a living union with Christ. Abiding in Him is the necessary condition for bearing spiritual fruit.

subject: CRS
text: Two of the seven disciples chosen to serve tables in the early church were
explanation: Acts 6:5 lists the seven men chosen to serve tables as Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas. Both Parmenas and Timon are genuinely among the seven. Andrew was an apostle, not one of the seven table-servers, making option D incorrect. Options B and C contain names not found in the Acts 6 list of the seven.

subject: CRS
text: "Give me also this power, that anyone on whom I lay my hands may receive the Holy Spirit". The speaker of this statement was
explanation: Acts 8:18-20 records Simon the Sorcerer offering money to purchase the power of the Holy Spirit, prompting Peter to rebuke him sharply. Peter declared that Simon's heart was not right before God and that he was 'in the gall of bitterness and in the bond of iniquity.' The attempt to buy God's gift for personal profit reveals Simon as fraudulent — seeking to use divine power for self-serving and deceptive purposes.

subject: CRS
text: At the Beautiful Gate, Peter and John healed a man who was
explanation: Acts 3:2 describes a man who was lame from birth being carried daily to the Beautiful Gate of the temple to beg. When Peter and John passed by, Peter commanded him in the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth to walk. The man's feet and ankles were immediately strengthened, and he entered the temple walking, leaping, and praising God.

subject: CRS
text: The people referred to as pillars of the Jerusalem church, who gave Barnabas and Paul the right hand of fellowship were
explanation: Galatians 2:9 identifies James, Cephas (Peter), and John as those 'reputed to be pillars' of the Jerusalem church who extended to Paul and Barnabas the right hand of fellowship. Paul recounts this meeting to establish the recognition his apostleship received from the original Jerusalem leadership. These three were the acknowledged leaders of the mother church in Jerusalem.

subject: CRS
text: The condition for justification according to Paul is
explanation: Paul's central teaching on justification is that a person is justified by faith in Jesus Christ, not by works of the law (Galatians 2:16, Romans 3:22). The object of saving faith is specifically Christ and His atoning work on the cross. No human merit, spiritual gift, or law-keeping can serve as the condition for justification — only trust in the crucified and risen Christ achieves this.

subject: CRS
text: On which of the following grounds will faith be null and the promise void?

options.A: Faith is a necessary condition for justification
options.C: Salvation comes through adherence to the law

explanation: Romans 4:14 states that if those who adhere to the law are the heirs, faith is null and the promise is void. Paul argues that the promise was given by grace through faith, not through legal compliance. If salvation depended on keeping the law, faith would be meaningless and the promise God made to Abraham would be invalidated, since no one can perfectly keep the law.

subject: CRS
text: According to the Letter of Paul to the Romans, Christians are to yield themselves to God as men who

options.A: have been raised from death to life
options.B: no longer live by the works of law
options.D: have listened to the gospel of Christ

explanation: Romans 6:13 exhorts believers to yield themselves to God 'as those who have been brought from death to life, and your members to God as instruments for righteousness.' Paul grounds the ethical call to holy living in the believer's new status — having died to sin and been raised to new life in Christ through baptism. This resurrection identity is the basis for yielding oneself completely to God.

subject: CRS
text: God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world in the likeness of sinful flesh in order

options.B: that the law might be fulfilled
options.C: that man may walk in the flesh

explanation: Romans 8:3-4 states that God sent His Son in the likeness of sinful flesh as a sin offering to condemn sin in the flesh, 'in order that the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit.' The incarnation and atoning work of Christ achieved what the law alone could not — the fulfillment of God's righteous demands on humanity.

subject: CRS
text: "...at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord..." The statement above demonstrates the Lordship of Jesus over
explanation: Philippians 2:10-11 declares that at the name of Jesus every knee will bow — in heaven, on earth, and under the earth — and every tongue confess His Lordship. The threefold cosmic scope (heavenly beings, earthly creatures, and those below the earth) emphasises that Christ's Lordship extends over the entirety of creation, not just believers or any particular group. This is a universal declaration of His sovereignty.

subject: CRS
text: Paul told the Corinthians that if anyone has caused pain to the church, he should be
explanation: 2 Corinthians 2:7 records Paul urging the Corinthians to 'forgive and comfort' the person who had caused pain, 'or he may be overwhelmed by excessive sorrow.' Paul had earlier recommended discipline, but now calls for restoration — demonstrating the full cycle of church discipline: rebuke, repentance, and then forgiveness and comfort. This reflects the restorative rather than purely punitive purpose of church discipline.

subject: CRS
text: The greatest spiritual gift to seek for is
explanation: 1 Corinthians 12:31 urges believers to eagerly desire the higher gifts, and then Paul proceeds in chapter 13 to show that love is the greatest of all. While spiritual gifts such as tongues, healing, and miracles are valuable, love surpasses them all in importance. Paul declares that without love, all other gifts and achievements amount to nothing (1 Cor 13:1-3).

subject: CRS
text: One of the civic responsibilities Christians are commanded to carry out is
explanation: Romans 13:1-7 and 1 Peter 2:13-17 command Christians to be subject to governing authorities as a civic responsibility. Paul teaches that governing authorities are instituted by God and that obedience to them is a Christian duty. Submission to lawful authority is thus a concrete civic responsibility for believers, distinct from purely spiritual or community obligations.

subject: CRS
text: In the course of his ministry, Paul worked night and day so that

options.A: he might not be a burden to anyone
options.C: he could raise money for the work of the gospel
options.D: he could pay the debt he was owing the brethren

explanation: 1 Thessalonians 2:9 records Paul reminding the Thessalonians that he laboured night and day so as not to be a burden to any of them while he proclaimed the gospel. Paul's manual labour alongside his ministry demonstrated his sincere motives and his refusal to exploit those he served. His self-support was a model of ministerial integrity and genuine care for his converts.

subject: CRS
text: Those who have fallen asleep before the coming of the Lord are those who
explanation: 1 Thessalonians 4:13-15 uses 'those who have fallen asleep' as a euphemism for Christians who have died physically before the Lord's return. Paul reassures the Thessalonians that these deceased believers will not be disadvantaged at the second coming — they will rise first, before the living are caught up. The phrase reflects the early Christian view of physical death as a sleep from which believers will be awakened.

subject: CRS
text: James enjoins Christians to remain impartial by

options.A: showing love to all without distinction

explanation: James 2:1-4 rebukes partiality in the assembly, particularly the practice of honouring the rich and dishonoring the poor. James calls Christians to treat all brethren equally, regardless of their social or economic status. Playing favourites in the church violates the royal law of love and demonstrates that faith without impartial love is inconsistent with genuine Christianity.

subject: CRS
text: According to James, the man who prays to God and at the same time doubts is
explanation: James 1:6-8 describes the person who doubts as like a wave of the sea, driven and tossed by the wind. Such a person is explicitly called 'double-minded' and 'unstable in all his ways.' James teaches that effective prayer requires wholehearted faith without wavering — the doubting person is divided in loyalty and commitment, which is why he should not expect to receive anything from the Lord.

subject: CRS
text: Peter admonishes Christians that suffering for Christ's sake brings God's
explanation: 1 Peter 4:14 states, 'If you are insulted for the name of Christ, you are blessed, for the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you.' Peter encourages suffering Christians by assuring them that bearing reproach for Christ's sake is accompanied by God's blessing and the presence of His Spirit. This reframes suffering not as punishment or failure but as a mark of honour and divine favour.

subject: CRS
text: "Behold, I have given you every plant yielding seed which is upon the face of all the earth, and every tree with seed in its fruit; ..." In the statement above, God made all provision for man to
explanation: In Genesis 1:29, God gave humanity every seed-bearing plant and fruit tree for food. This provision was made so that man would have enough food to eat. The emphasis in the passage is on God's generosity in supplying mankind's nutritional needs from the very beginning of creation.

subject: CRS
text: God told Noah that "Whoever sheds the blood of man by man, shall his blood be shed; ..." because

options.A: man is made in God's image
options.C: blood is the life of man

explanation: Genesis 9:6 explicitly states the reason: "for in the image of God has God made mankind." Because human beings bear the image of God, taking a human life is an affront to God Himself. This divine image gives human life its unique, sacred value.

subject: CRS
text: What did God promise the Israelites after their rescue from Babylon to make them fear Him forever?

options.A: A new heart and a new life
options.B: A new covenant and a new spirit

explanation: Jeremiah 32:39 records God's promise: "I will give them one heart and one way, that they may fear me forever." This unity of heart and purpose would ensure that the restored people remained faithful to God and did not repeat the idolatry that led to their captivity.

subject: CRS
text: The Lord appeared to Abraham and promised his descendants the land of the Canaanites at
explanation: Genesis 12:6–7 records that Abram traveled through the land and came to the site of the great tree (oak) of Moreh at Shechem. There the Lord appeared to him and promised to give the land to his offspring. This was the first divine land promise made at a specific geographical location.

subject: CRS
text: When Joseph narrated his second dream, he was rebuked by Jacob because the dream would

options.A: make his brothers all the more jealous
options.B: subject his parents to his authority
options.D: send the family to Egypt as slaves

explanation: In Genesis 37:9–10, Joseph's second dream showed the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing down to him. Jacob rebuked him, saying, "Shall I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow ourselves to the ground before you?" The dream implied that even Joseph's parents would be subject to him, which Jacob found presumptuous.

subject: CRS
text: Deborah was a judge when Israel was under the yoke of
explanation: Judges 4:1–3 records that after Ehud died, the Israelites did evil and the LORD sold them into the hands of Jabin king of Canaan, whose commander was Sisera. It was during this Canaanite oppression that Deborah rose to judge Israel and eventually led the people to victory.

subject: CRS
text: When Jacob woke from sleep on his way to Haran, he was afraid and named the place Bethel because he believed that the

options.A: place was a part of heaven
options.B: place had a ladder that led to heaven
options.C: angels of the Lord lived there
options.D: place was the gate of heaven

explanation: Genesis 28:17 records Jacob's words: "How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven." Jacob's fear and awe arose from his conviction that the place where the ladder reached to heaven was the very gateway between earth and the divine realm.

subject: CRS
text: God rejected the sons of Eli from being priest over His people because they were
explanation: 1 Samuel 2:22–25 shows that Hophni and Phinehas were rejected because they slept with the women who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting (immorality) and refused to heed their father's correction (sedition/rebelliousness). Their dual sins of sexual immorality and defiant refusal of correction led to God's judgment against the house of Eli.

subject: CRS
text: The final consequence of Saul's disobedience to God was his
explanation: 1 Samuel 31:4–6 records that when Saul was badly wounded and his armour-bearer refused to kill him, Saul fell on his own sword and died on the battlefield against the Philistines at Mount Gilboa. His death in the war front was the ultimate consequence of his long pattern of disobedience to God's commands.

subject: CRS
text: God forgave David when he took Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah because

options.A: the child he had with her died
options.C: David fasted and laid upon the ground
options.D: David was a man after God's heart

explanation: 2 Samuel 12:13 shows that immediately after Nathan confronted him, David said "I have sinned against the LORD," and Nathan replied "The LORD also has put away your sin." God's forgiveness came because David genuinely accepted his guilt without excuse or deflection. His confession was the turning point that secured divine forgiveness.

subject: CRS
text: Abner sent messengers to David at Hebron because he wanted to

options.A: bring over all Israel to David
options.C: spy on Hebron and capture it later

explanation: 2 Samuel 3:12 records that Abner sent messengers to David saying, "Make your covenant with me, and behold, my hand shall be with you to bring over all Israel to you." Abner's goal was to transfer his allegiance and bring the northern tribes under David's rule, consolidating David's kingship over all Israel.

subject: CRS
text: Where did the Lord appear to Solomon in a dream?
explanation: 1 Kings 3:4–5 states that King Solomon went to Gibeon to offer sacrifices, for it was the most important high place, and there the LORD appeared to him in a dream at night. It was during this dream at Gibeon that Solomon made his famous request for wisdom to govern God's people.

subject: CRS
text: The division of David's kingdom was a direct consequence of
explanation: 1 Kings 12 shows that when the people asked Rehoboam to lighten their burdens, he rejected the counsel of the elders and instead threatened to be even harsher. This unwise decision directly triggered the revolt that split the kingdom into Israel and Judah. Had Rehoboam listened wisely, the kingdom might have remained united.

subject: CRS
text: Naaman's leprosy was transferred to Gehazi as a result of

options.A: the testimony of the maid of Israel
options.C: the gifts he received from Naaman
options.D: the bath Gehazi took in the river Jordan

explanation: 2 Kings 5:27 records Elisha's pronouncement: "Therefore the leprosy of Naaman shall cling to you and to your descendants forever." This judgment came because Gehazi had disobeyed both Elisha's instructions and God's standards by running after Naaman to deceitfully collect gifts in Elisha's name. His covetousness represented a fundamental act of disobedience.

subject: CRS
text: The prayer of Elijah in the name of the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob brought down fire from heaven which

options.A: killed all the prophets of Baal
options.B: destroyed the demonic spirits of Jezebel

explanation: 1 Kings 18:38 records: "Then the fire of the LORD fell and consumed the burnt offering and the wood and the stones and the dust, and licked up the water that was in the trench." The fire's first and direct effect was consuming the sacrifice on the altar, which dramatically proved that the LORD, not Baal, was the true God of Israel.

subject: CRS
text: The reforms of King Josiah were made possible by the

options.A: discovery of the book of the law
options.D: destruction of the chariot of Ahaz

explanation: 2 Kings 22:8 records that the high priest Hilkiah told Shaphan the scribe, "I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD." When this book was read to King Josiah, he tore his robes in repentance and launched his sweeping religious reforms. The discovery of the law book was the catalyst for his entire reform movement.

subject: CRS
text: As Nehemiah embarked on reconstruction of Jerusalem, he faced threats and opposition from
explanation: The book of Nehemiah repeatedly identifies Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite as the chief opponents of Jerusalem's reconstruction (Neh 2:10, 19; 4:1–9; 6:1–14). They mocked, threatened, and plotted against Nehemiah's work at every turn. Option A contains OCR-damaged text and is not a real historical reference.

subject: CRS
text: Amos told the Israelites that they have turned justice into
explanation: Amos 6:12 asks rhetorically, "Have you turned justice into poison and the fruit of righteousness into wormwood?" Wormwood is an extremely bitter plant used figuratively to describe how Israel perverted justice, making what should have been righteous and sweet into something bitterly corrupt and toxic.

subject: CRS
text: Hosea compared Judah's love for the Lord to the morning cloud because it

options.A: goes away early like the dew
options.C: goes up to God like a smoke

explanation: Hosea 6:4 reads: "Your love is like a morning cloud, like the dew that goes early away." God laments that Judah's love for Him is fleeting and transient—just as morning clouds and dew quickly disappear under the morning sun, so their devotion evaporated without producing lasting faithfulness.

subject: CRS
text: The attribute of God which Isaiah emphasizes most is His
explanation: Isaiah 6:3 presents the seraphim crying to one another, "Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory." This triple repetition of "holy" (the Hebrew superlative) is the defining vision of Isaiah's call. Throughout his book, Isaiah repeatedly uses the title "the Holy One of Israel," making holiness the central divine attribute he proclaims.

subject: CRS
text: "...even though you make prayers, I will not listen..." Why will God the Holy One of Israel not listen?

options.A: because they sacrifice with incense to Him
options.B: because God detests their burnt offerings of rams
options.C: because their hands are full of blood
options.D: because their new moon gifts are a burden to Him

explanation: Isaiah 1:15 records God saying, "When you spread out your hands, I will hide my eyes from you; even though you make many prayers, I will not listen; your hands are full of blood." The specific reason God refuses to hear their prayers is the blood on their hands—the violence, injustice, and oppression they perpetrate against the vulnerable.

subject: CRS
text: The vision of the dry bones of Ezekiel portrays
explanation: Ezekiel 37:11–14 interprets the valley of dry bones as a vision of national restoration: the bones represent the house of Israel who say "Our bones are dried up, and our hope is lost." God's act of breathing life into the bones symbolizes His promise to restore exiled Israel to their land. The vision's primary message is one of hope and resurrection for the nation.

subject: CRS
text: What was Peter's reaction when he saw the great shoal of fish he and his partners caught?
explanation: Luke 5:8 records that when Simon Peter saw the miraculous catch, "he fell down at Jesus' knees, saying, 'Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord.'" The overwhelming display of divine power caused Peter to become acutely aware of his own sinfulness and unworthiness before the holy presence of Jesus.

subject: CRS
text: In the feeding of the five thousand, according to Matthew, the first thing that Jesus did was to

options.A: order the loaves and fish to be brought to Him
options.B: break the loaves and fish brought to Him
options.C: look up to heaven and pray to God
options.D: give thanks to God and have the fish and loaves shared

explanation: Matthew 14:19 states that Jesus "looked up to heaven and said a blessing" before breaking and distributing the loaves. The very first action described is His looking up to heaven, which was the posture of prayer and blessing that preceded all other actions in the miracle. This highlights Jesus' dependence on the Father.

subject: CRS
text: According to John, the six stone jars of water which Jesus turned into wine were kept there for
explanation: John 2:6 explains that the six stone water jars "were there for the Jewish rites of purification, each holding twenty or thirty gallons." These vessels were used for the ceremonial washing of hands and utensils required by Jewish law. Jesus chose to use these purification jars, perhaps symbolically transforming the old covenant's external rituals into something new and joyful.

subject: CRS
text: When the man with an unclean spirit in the region of the Gerasenes saw Jesus, he

options.A: acknowledged Him as the son of the most High God
options.B: pleaded to be cast into the Abyss
options.C: requested to follow Him as a disciple
options.D: pleaded that a miracle be performed to cleanse him

explanation: Mark 5:7 (and Luke 8:28) records the man with the unclean spirit crying out with a loud voice, "What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God?" Even the demons recognized and acknowledged Jesus' divine identity, demonstrating that His authority extends over the supernatural realm.

subject: CRS
text: "To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it has not been given ..." This statement was made after Jesus had told the parable of the
explanation: Matthew 13:3–11 shows that after Jesus told the parable of the sower (v. 3–9), the disciples asked why He spoke in parables (v. 10), and He responded that to them it had been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of heaven (v. 11). This statement about kingdom secrets follows directly after the parable of the sower.

subject: CRS
text: The parable of the lost sheep portrays the shepherd as being
explanation: Luke 15:4–7 and Matthew 18:12–14 portray a shepherd who leaves ninety-nine sheep to search diligently for the one lost sheep, and then rejoices greatly when it is found. This extravagant effort and joy reveals the shepherd's deep love for each individual sheep. Jesus uses this image to show God's loving care for every lost sinner.

subject: CRS
text: "... Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, ..." This statement shows that Jesus

options.A: does not want Christians to be rich
options.C: wants Christians to seek first the kingdom of God
options.D: regards poverty as a condition for the kingdom of God

explanation: In context, Matthew 6:19–33 moves from the warning against earthly treasures to the climactic command of verse 33: "But seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness." Jesus' teaching is not a prohibition of all possessions but a call to right priorities—putting God's kingdom above material accumulation.

subject: CRS
text: Jesus commissioned the twelve disciples charging them to

options.A: go to all the world and preach
options.B: go to the lost sheep of the house of Israel
options.C: receive hospitality and wages for their labour
options.D: perform healing miracles among the Samaritans

explanation: Matthew 10:6 records Jesus' explicit instruction: "Go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel." This initial commissioning was geographically and ethnically restricted to Israel—the universal mission to all nations came later after the resurrection. The twelve were first sent to their own people.

subject: CRS
text: According to Mark, after Jesus had triumphantly entered Jerusalem, He

options.B: went to Bethany with the twelve
options.C: wept over the city and prophesied its destruction
options.D: addressed the Pharisees who had asked Him to rebuke His disciples

explanation: Mark 11:11 uniquely records that after the triumphal entry, Jesus went into the temple, looked around at everything, and then "as it was already late, he went out to Bethany with the twelve." Mark's account differs from Matthew and Luke by showing that the temple cleansing happened the next day, after Jesus returned from Bethany.

subject: CRS
text: According to Matthew, the inscription over Jesus when he was crucified was

options.A: "This is the King of the Jews"
options.C: "Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews"
options.D: "This is Jesus King of the Jews"

explanation: Matthew 27:37 records that above Jesus' head they placed the written charge against him: "This is Jesus, the King of the Jews." Each Gospel has a slightly different wording of the inscription; Matthew's version includes the word "This is" followed by Jesus' name and the title, making option D the correct answer specifically for Matthew's account.

subject: CRS
text: Which disciple asked Jesus, "Lord, we do not know where you are going; how can we know the way?"
explanation: John 14:5 records that Thomas said to Jesus, "Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?" This question prompted Jesus' famous declaration: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life." Thomas's honest, searching question became the occasion for one of Jesus' most profound self-revelations.

subject: CRS
text: "...For he was numbered among us, and was allotted his share in this ministry..." What happened to the person referred to in the statement above?
explanation: Acts 1:16–18 identifies the person as Judas Iscariot and records that he "acquired a field with the reward of his wickedness, and falling headlong he burst open in the middle and all his bowels gushed out." Matthew 27:5 explicitly states he hanged himself. The passage is about Judas, who committed suicide after betraying Jesus.

subject: CRS
text: The disciples in the early church chose the seven deacons in order to

options.A: solve the problem of murmuring by the Hellenists
options.B: have adequate daily distribution of food
options.C: take proper care of the neglected widows
options.D: devote themselves to prayer and teaching the word

explanation: Acts 6:2–4 shows that the twelve summoned the congregation and said it was not right for them to neglect the word of God to serve tables; therefore they appointed the seven so that the apostles themselves could "devote ourselves to prayer and to the ministry of the word." The deacons' appointment freed the apostles for their primary calling.

subject: CRS
text: The sound which came from heaven on the day of Pentecost is likened to that of
explanation: Acts 2:2 records: "And suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting." The sound was not actual wind but was compared to it. This rushing mighty wind symbolized the powerful, life-giving presence of the Holy Spirit coming upon the believers.

subject: CRS
text: What were the major issues of controversy that led to the appearance of Paul and Barnabas before the Council in Jerusalem?

options.A: Circumcision and food offered to idols
options.B: Unchastity and the Law of Moses
options.C: Circumcision and the Law of Moses
options.D: The Law of Moses and Pollution by idols

explanation: Acts 15:1 and 15:5 identify the two main controversies: some men from Judea taught that Gentiles must be circumcised (v. 1), and some Pharisees insisted they must also keep the law of Moses (v. 5). These twin issues—circumcision and the law of Moses—were the specific points of dispute that necessitated the Jerusalem Council.

subject: CRS
text: According to John, the distinctive quality of the disciples of Jesus Christ is

options.C: laying down their lives for others

explanation: John 13:35 records Jesus' words: "By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another." Love for one another is the identifying mark—the external proof—that distinguishes Jesus' followers. It is what makes the Christian community recognizable to the watching world.

subject: CRS
text: God has extended the promise He made to Abraham to all those who share his faith based on
explanation: Romans 4:16 states: "That is why it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his offspring—not only to the adherent of the law but also to the one who shares the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all." The promise extends to all who share Abraham's faith, which itself was credited to him as righteousness (v. 3)—hence righteousness of faith.

subject: CRS
text: According to Galatians, what makes all believers sons of God is
explanation: Galatians 3:26 states plainly: "For in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God, through faith." The specific mechanism Paul identifies is faith in Christ Jesus—not law-keeping, ancestry, or any other factor. It is trust in Christ that unites believers to Him and makes them children of God.

subject: CRS
text: Speaking in tongues according to Paul, is valid where

options.A: there is one with the gift of interpretation
options.B: the Holy Spirit is present during worship
options.D: those speaking in tongues are many

explanation: 1 Corinthians 14:27–28 regulates tongues in public worship: "If any speak in a tongue, let there be only two or at most three, and each in turn, and let someone interpret. But if there is no one to interpret, let each of them keep silent in church." The essential condition Paul requires for valid public tongues-speaking is the presence of someone with the gift of interpretation.

subject: CRS
text: Paul says that anyone who does acts of mercy should do so with
explanation: Romans 12:8 lists various gifts and their proper exercise: "the one who does acts of mercy, with cheerfulness." Paul emphasizes that merciful deeds should flow from a joyful, willing heart—not from obligation or grudging duty. God loves a cheerful giver, and this principle extends to all acts of compassionate ministry.

subject: CRS
text: Paul advises Christians to obey authorities because

options.B: rulers want people to obey them

explanation: Romans 13:1 states: "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God." Paul's foundational reason for Christian obedience to government is that rulers exercise a divinely delegated authority—they have a divine mandate from God, not merely human power.

subject: CRS
text: The second coming of Christ shall be recognized by:

options.C: earthquakes and wars, darkness on earth

explanation: 1 Thessalonians 4:16 describes the second coming: "For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God." The first and most distinctive feature Paul mentions is the authoritative "cry of command," which initiates all the other events of Christ's return.

subject: CRS
text: "And the dead in Christ will rise first; then we who are alive, who are left, shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air" From the statement above, Paul's belief was that he would;

options.A: live to witness the second coming of Christ
options.B: have died before the second coming of Christ
options.C: resurrect at the second coming of Christ
options.D: have been in paradise before the second coming

explanation: 1 Thessalonians 4:15 and 17 show Paul including himself among "we who are alive, who are left until the coming of the Lord." By using first-person plural "we," Paul indicates his personal expectation of being alive at the Parousia—he believed he would live to witness the second coming of Christ rather than die beforehand.

subject: CRS
text: According to James, whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point

options.B: has become guilty of all of it
options.C: will be punished for a little while

explanation: James 2:10 states: "For whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of breaking all of it." James teaches that God's law is a unified whole—breaking any part of it makes one a lawbreaker before the divine Lawgiver. This underscores the impossibility of achieving righteousness through law-keeping.

subject: CRS
text: James in his Epistle urged the brethren to count their trials as joy because the testing of their faith produces;
explanation: James 1:3 explains: "for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness." Trials are to be welcomed because they have a productive purpose—the refining of faith under pressure results in steadfastness (endurance, perseverance), which is the mature Christian virtue that enables believers to stand firm through ongoing difficulties.

subject: CRS
text: According to Peter, if a Christian suffers for his faith, he should not be
explanation: 1 Peter 4:16 states: "Yet if anyone suffers as a Christian, let him not be ashamed, but let him glorify God in that name." Peter addresses the temptation to feel shame when suffering for one's faith. Rather than being ashamed, the suffering Christian should glorify God, because suffering for Christ is an honor, not a disgrace.

subject: CRS
text: Peter says that Christians would put to silence the ignorance of foolish men by
explanation: 1 Peter 2:15 states: "For this is the will of God, that by doing good you should put to silence the ignorance of foolish people." The most powerful response to false accusations and slander is not argument but righteous conduct. A consistent life of good deeds silences critics more effectively than any verbal defense.
